s1_b1: Stand on a frozen lake, perfectly still. Throw a heavy bag forward.
s1_b2: You slide backward. You didn't push the ground. The bag did nothing to the world but leave your hands.
s1_b3: Before: nothing moving. After: two things moving, opposite ways. Where did that motion come from?
s1_b4: Add it up the right way — motion as momentum, with direction. Forward counts as plus. Backward as minus.
s1_b5: The bag's forward momentum and your backward momentum are equal and opposite. They cancel.
s1_b6: Total momentum before: zero. Total after: still zero. Nothing was created. It only got shared.
s1_b7: This holds whenever no outside force interferes. A system left to itself. Isolated.
s1_b8: And it's just Newton's third law, looked at over time. Every push has an equal, opposite push back.
s1_b9: It's how a rocket climbs. Throw exhaust down hard; the rocket is thrown up just as hard.
s1_b10: And how collisions work. Two carts meet. Whatever one gains, the other gives up. The total never changes.
s1_b11: Inside the boundary, momentum is currency. It moves around. It is never spent.
s1_b12: Setting total momentum before equal to total momentum after, and solving for an unknown velocity in a collision or recoil — that's yours.
